speaker: interviewer
text: You arrived to the gates of Dachau. Take us through visually what you saw when you arrived. And what did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, the first thing we saw before we went to the gates of Dachau was this train load of bodies. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was traumatic enough without having gone into the-- into the compound itself. We got to the gate. And this is what the gate looked like when we arrived. And that was the-- actually, it wasn't the gate that most of the prisoners were taken into. It was another gate on the other side of the camp. And having been-- seen these bodies on this train was-- was pretty traumatic. But then going into the camp, to just-- to see this unbelievable scene of-- of death. It-- it was the 29th of April. And it was- had been cold, so we didn't-- it wasn't as terrible smelling as it might have been. But the smell-- you could smell death in this camp. And as I mentioned, having met this Belgian officer and was taken around a camp and to see one absolutely revolting scene after another-- the barracks where they were-- the prisoners were. There were-- a lot of them were so sick, they couldn't move hardly. Some who were able to move were so emaciated from the lack of sustenance-- and the-- the piles of bodies all over the camp. The-- the crematorium, as I said, that had this stack of bodies that hadn't-- they hadn't been able to-- to burn. It turned out that they'd run out of fuel. And the train load of people that were out on the tracks outside the compound were brought there, presumably to be cremated. Some had, I guess, had died in other camps, in maybe in some of the satellites. It turns out that all of these major camps, like Dachau and Auschwitz and Belsen-Bergen, and-- all of those had supplemental camps. Dachau had some 110 supplemental camps that had smaller amounts of prisoners there, mostly work details in the local areas that needed-- like in ammunition-producing factories and had these small camps that had a few hundred folks. But Dachau itself had thousands-- thousands.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as I said, that, you know, a young 20-year-old has never even imagined anything like this. How-- how could this be? How-- how in the world could people be treated this way? How is it possible? And to have the-- the-- the revolting and shocking and gut-wrenching feeling come over us to see something like that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after a time, you know, many of the prisoners-- we talked with them. And-- and we-- we tried to tell them in-- in our broken German-- a lot of them-- most of them were Jewish. But there were a lot of other nationalities there, too. There were some Russian and Polish and French, all kinds of nationalities in this camp. And I was able to speak some German, so I was able to converse somewhat. And the Belgian officer I was with spoke quite good German and French and Belgian. And [LAUGHS] he was able to translate for me. And I tried to tell the people that I talked to through him that we were there as liberators, although we had to keep them in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please understand that we're not going to keep you in here forever. And we're not going to treat you like the Germans did. You're going to be free one of these days, but we've got to wait till people come from the rear-- the medical people and the people with food for you. And you're going to be spoon fed for a while, 'cause you-- we can't give you a banquet right off or you'll die. And so I think the word spread that we were really friends, and we were going to let them go. Did you know that they cannot be fed with regular food? How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we were told that. Do not. When we got into the camp, the battalion commander that was-- came up behind us-- in fact, there were a lot-- awful lot of folks came up behind us. Our job was to-- to get into the camp, keep prisoners in, don't-- don't feed them. And we were told that. Don't feed them, because they haven't had any food for a long period of time-- enough. They had enough to keep them barely alive, those who hadn't died. So we were told, do not feed them. And do not let them out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, I wanted to ask you, in the cattle cars, in the boxcars that you found outside, did you find anyone alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, yes. I didn't know that at the time there was anybody alive, because we went into the camp. That was our job, to get inside and close the gate and keep prisoners in, and try to comfort them as much as we could. And it turned out that one person was alive in those 40 cars. I don't know how many hundreds of dead bodies were in those cars. Turned out one of the sergeants heard a moan. And he called the battalion commander-- I think he's Don-- [? Donnard-- ?] he was colonel at the time, Colonel [? Donnard. ?] And a tank destroyer captain came. And the sergeant said, I heard-- I heard somebody moan. And so they went and found this one live person under a bunch of bodies. And apparently, he survived. And I understand he's still alive. He's my age [LAUGHS] now. He was a fairly young person when they got him out. Of course, he survived because he was under these bodies and-- and stayed warm enough not to freeze to death in that terrible transport of-- across-- from wherever it came. I don't know where it came from. It may have been from another camp that didn't have a crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, I wanted to ask whether you know any Jewish soldiers that were in your unit at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Bloss, ?] I believe was-- was a Jewish fellow. And he was-- he was one of them that was killed in that patrol that I talked about earlier. He was a stretcher bearer for the fellow that had his foot blown off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know any of the-- of your liberators that were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm trying to think of when we went to San Francisco, whether there were some. I believe, yes. I've forgotten their names. I think they'd be on the list in that booklet we got. Yeah, there were. Probably [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a dozen or so of the liberators were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the time you went into the camp, did you notice their behavior when they found out that these are Jewish prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: My company, no. There was no-- no reaction other than revulsion. And we didn't know that they were Jews in that camp at first. In fact, I wasn't sure until sometime later that that was a part of the extermination of the Jews, that they were being summarily [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] put to death or starved to death or executed or-- or experimented on. And all these things, I didn't know at the time. All I knew was here's thousands of people that had-- a lot of wrong had been done to them. But I didn't know what-- their nationalities. I learned later that there were all kinds of-- the population in Dachau was mostly Jewish. But as I said, there were French, and there were this Belgian captain. And there were Polish, and there were-- there were Polish Jews and displaced persons and political prisoners, Germans who were in there. In fact, Dachau, as I mentioned earlier, it was started in 1933, primarily to-- to put homosexuals and-- and people against the movement that Hitler was sponsoring and misfits. And all were gathered up and as early as 1933 were in that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came in, were you aware of any homosexuals--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or groups of homosexuals?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I-- really, I didn't-- I didn't really know about homosexuality in those days. I didn't-- I didn't know anything about it. That's something that I've learned later in life that there is such a thing and-- and the movement by-- by homosexuals to-- to get the people to understand them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with all these bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did nothing. We-- we didn't try to do anything with them. They-- they were all given, I guess, their burials later. We were only-- my company was only there for two days. In fact, we left the next day, the 30th of April. Because we still had combat to-- to go through. And we still had to go attack Munich, which was about nine miles away. So we were relieved of our duties there at Dachau and taken-- other units took over from us. And we gathered and took off for-- for Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And by the time we got there, we didn't really have very much of a-- of a combat situation. Most of the Germans had fled south, because it was obvious that the war was nearly over. And there weren't going to be too many more battles. The-- it became pointless to a lot of the Germans that, why do we continue doing this? But in spite of that, there were a lot of 'em that-- that fled south and holed up in the Alps in Austria. Did you find more prisoners when you went to Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? Tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them were actually just waiting for us. Those who decided not to continue fighting were waiting there on the outskirts of Munich. So we had thousands of German prisoners to take care of. Of course, now my-- my company is back with the rest of the battalion. And our whole unit is heading in that-- in that direction. So we just took them over and sent them back to the rear. And of course, there's a lot of people behind us that took on that responsibility to take care of the prisoners. Yeah, we-- we-- we found thousands of-- of Germans just sitting by-- beside the road with their weapons down on the ground. And they weren't-- they didn't want to fight anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about prisoners German-- from German camps? From concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we did not find any POWs ourselves-- my unit did not. There were, of course, some that other units found. But, I-- I, myself and my company or my battalion never-- never found prisoners of war. We did-- our units, in addition to Dachau, repatriated a number of prisoners. In fact, this book shows some of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: From where?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It was north of Dachau. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's in this book. I can't think of the name. [? Eschweiler? ?] I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jews? Jews? Non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some Jews, some non-Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, women and children and men-- mostly-- mostly women and children who were-- they weren't treated as badly as those in the-- in the concentration camps. It was-- in one case, there was a-- a building that-- that they were actually housed in and guarded by a few German soldiers. They had not been treated that badly.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Dachau, the-- the bodies and the prisoners were men, women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Men and women-- the women were segregated from the men. But there were men and women and-- and young people, children, some children. There were several children, corpses of children in-- in Dachau. And I think the-- the camp seemed to be split in-- in two, with-- with the women on this side and the men on this side. And then they had, just inside this gate that I showed you, were some buildings that I guess the more elite prisoners were kept, like this Belgian officer and some others-- some other officers. There was a Frenchman and a-- a Polish officer were in-- housed in this. And they were treated a little less severe than the others.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your unit, how far into Germany did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went-- we actually went on a kind of a right angle [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] trail, Wurzburg. And then we turned south and went through Nuremberg. So we went kind of-- kind of like a-- a right turn and went to Munich and on south of Munich, all the way into Austria. We ended the war actually in southern Austria. Our job in Austria was to go into the Alps and ferret out those Germans that had fled south. Our intelligence said that they are holed up in the Alps, in various places in the Alps, and go find them, most of all, to tell them the war's over. [LAUGHS] Don't fight it anymore. You can go home. So we found a few.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you come across other prisoner camps, concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, that was the only one. Except maybe this building I was talking about, that when we liberated it, it was-- it was something like the-- the supplemental camps. This was fairly far north of-- actually, north of Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you write home about Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I didn't. I-- in fact, I didn't talk about that at all to anybody for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess I didn't-- I didn't want to-- to renew the-- the thought of it. I didn't want to describe it to anybody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My former wife I felt wouldn't understand anyway. And the only time I ever talked about it was with fellow military people. I was in the reserve. When I got home, I-- I joined the Army Reserve. And of course, we talked about it. But I never did-- did really talk about it at all. I had a lot of nightmares about it for about 10 years, nightmares about Dachau, about the experience there, and about some of the people that were in my family in that unit were killed. I had nightmares about that and nightmares about the-- the real gut-wrenching experiences that I'd had. I had nightmares for a long time. But I didn't really want to discuss it with anybody. I wanted to have that as a-- as a terrible experience that I now was through with. I survived the war. And I'll go on with what I wanted to do, which was to become an engineer and go back to school. And of course, I got married immediately after getting back home. My wife was then in Tacoma, Washington. And that's where I was married to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met her in Tulsa, Oklahoma, when I was in Camp Gruber, which was in Muskogee-- outside of Muskogee, Oklahoma. One weekend, I had a leave to go up to Tulsa. And I-- I, along with a couple other officers, went up there. And one of them had met a girl in Tulsa earlier. And so I went with them. And we-- we hired a taxi, I guess, and went all the way from Muskogee to-- [LAUGHS] to Tulsa in a taxi. And I met his girlfriend. And-- and she found a-- a-- a girlfriend for me and for the other fellow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we-- this was on a Saturday. And I was going to meet her. Gosh, what was her name? [? Ora Fay ?], [? Ora Fay Jones ?] was her name. And I was going to meet her on Sunday. But for some reason, she couldn't make it. And so this girl knew of another girl. Her name was Sally-- Sara Straton.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I met her on Sunday. And we went to a picnic out on the park in Tulsa and got rained on. And we went back to this girl's house and-- and had our uniforms pressed and-- [LAUGHS] because we got soaked. And that was my-- my meeting of Sally. And we had, I think two dates after that. I went up to Tulsa to a football game. Tulsa-- Tulsa High School, I guess, was playing some other team, and I-- no, it was Tulsa University was playing some other team. And so I went to the football game with her. And then one other date, and-- and I went overseas. And we promised to correspond.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we wrote letters back and forth. And that's when, really, our plan to get married when I got home happened. We didn't know each other from nothing. [LAUGHS] What was her full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sara Florence Straton.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever tell her about Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I-- I didn't think that she'd understand. Or-- or-- It turned out that-- that she wasn't particularly interested in-- in what I was interested in. And that's why she is my former wife, God rest her soul. She passed away. And that's another story. [SIGHS] So no, I didn't talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you settle right after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Married in Tacoma. Moved into my mother's house in North Hollywood, and that's where we lived from July-- well, we went on a honeymoon up in Northern California, and then drove-- my mother had given us their car. It was-- which was a '36 Dodge. That was a wedding present. So we drove that '36 Dodge around and drove to North Hollywood and lived in my mother's house. And then my sister and her husband, who had been married two months earlier, asked if they couldn't come and live in that same house-- asked my mother. And that turned out to be a terrible plan. We were at each other's throats all the time. And my wife, Sally, didn't help the situation any. She was a self-centered person. Anyway, we decided we've got to get out of there. So I applied at UCLA, because I was-- I was going to UCLA then, applied at UCLA for an apartment on the campus. There were a number of buildings that had been moved from Vanport near Portland and rebuilt on the campus of UCLA. And we were able to get an apartment in-- that would have been '46. I think it was about November of '46 we were able to get that apartment. So we only lived there in North Hollywood for a few months, a couple of months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remain in the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remained in the Reserve. When I-- when I was separated from the service at Camp Kilmer, I was asked if I wanted to stay in the Reserve. And I said I did-- I wanted to. So I-- I was assigned to a unit in Los Angeles at Fort MacArthur, actually. And so I stayed in-- in-- in the Reserve, actually until I retired in 1974.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came back with what rank?

speaker: interviewee
text: First lieutenant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you decorated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had the Silver Star and a Bronze Star and, of course, combat ribbons. I have a plaque on the wall down the hall that shows those ribbons. European Theater of Operations medal and Victory Medal, Occupation Medal, Good Conduct medal [LAUGHS] that I'd gotten earlier, and a Combat Infantry badge. I had all those-- those decorations.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you look at those decorations today, what do they mean to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They mean a great deal to me, particularly the Silver Star. As I said earlier, I-- I didn't think of myself as a hero. I thought I-- I got tired of being shot at. And so I was going to do something to-- to not be shot at anymore, you know, take-- take a positive approach, take the offensive and go capture some Germans. And whoever witnessed that thought I had done something pretty spectacular. And so I got awarded the medal. And it-- it means a great deal to me. I-- I was-- it-- it means that someone respected what I did, felt I-- I had some-- some substance. And so yeah, that means a lot. The Bronze Star also means a lot, because the battalion commander said, I am going to recommend you for the Bronze Star. And I hadn't thought I was high on the totem pole as far as he was concerned, but it turned out that I was. So that means a lot. And the whole-- all of those medals mean a-- mean a great deal. They're-- they're-- they're-- they're just medals. But they mean something to me, because they-- they renew an experience that I had that I think was favorable. So I think they-- they mean a-- the medals I got, some of those in that cabinet or in that shadow box, I got from ROTC. And they mean a lot. One was for wall scaling. [LAUGHS] We got a medal, because-- because we were first place in the timed wall scaling, where we had to go up a rope and climb over this wall. And we got over there first. And so I got a medal for that. And a medal for-- for sharp shooting-- and those are-- are very important to me, too. And my rank insignia means a lot to me, because I went from first lieutenant to bird colonel, albeit in the reserve. But I-- I got there. And I'm-- I'm pretty proud of that. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You retired as a colonel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in 1974.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I retired? [SIGHS] Well, from the military, I-- I actually was in the Reserve. And I-- I went back to school, still in the Army Reserve. But I went back to school and got my degree at UCLA.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what?

speaker: interviewee
text: In engineering-- I wanted to be an engineer when I went into-- into the service. And-- and I really wanted to come back to school. Of course, I had met this lady in Europe, in Salzburg. And-- and I was kind of torn about leaving her and going back home. But I had made a commitment to get married, and so I came back home. I went back to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's wait here for a minute. I want to hear your story. And we want to change tapes right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
